Cross-defendant Sandoval's motion to quash is granted. A cross-defendant may seek to quash defective service of the cross-complaint. (See CCP Sec. 418.10(e) [defendant or cross-defendant may move to quash].) The party opposing a motion to quash has the burden to establish the basis for jurisdiction. (See DVI, Inc. v. MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-02-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT LIEN TRAN'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT:

Defendant Lien Ngoc Tran's motion for sanctions pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure Section 128.7 is denied. To the extent that Defendant intended to file a separate motion to strike, it would be denied as untimely, since Tran has already filed a general denial, and did not meet and confer as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 435.5.
Under Section 128.7, an attorney who "present[s] to the court, whether by signing, filing, submitting, or later advocating, a pleading, petition, written notice of motion, or other similar paper" certifies that there is a legal and factual merit. "A claim is factually frivolous if it is not well grounded in fact and is legally frivolous if it is not warranted by existing law or a good faith argument for the extension, modification, or reversal of existing law. In either case, to obtain sanctions, the moving party must show the party's conduct in asserting the claim was objectively unreasonable. A claim is objectively unreasonable if any reasonable attorney would agree that it is totally and completely without merit." (Bucur v. Ahmad (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 175, 189.)
Defendant does not show that the first cause of action is factually frivolous. Defendant's argument that the claim for breach of contract is frivolous because the check he received and the policy are not attached is unsupported. The complaint factually alleges that Defendant was paid $9,853 (Compl., 11) and that the Medical Payments Coverage Trust Agreement requires reimbursement (Compl., 9). That is all that is required. Defendant's motion goes to the merits of his defenses, which have yet to be adjudicated. His argument that the dispute must be arbitrated is groundless, since he has never filed a motion to compel arbitration.
Defendant does not show that the second cause of action is legally frivolous. While Defendant is correct that courts have pointed out that the "phrase Unjust Enrichment does not describe a theory of recovery, but an effect," Defendant does not provide any authority holding that claims for unjust enrichment are frivolous. (See Lauriedale Associates, Ltd. v. Wilson (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 1439, 1448.) Rather, courts have explained that claims for unjust enrichment "actually seek . . . restitution[,] a term which modernly has been extended to include not only the restoration or giving back of something to its rightful owner, but indemnification such as appellants seek in this case." (Id.; see Melchior v. New Line Productions, Inc. (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 779, 793 ["Unjust enrichment is a general principle, underlying various legal doctrines and remedies, rather than a remedy itself. It is synonymous with restitution."].) "An individual is required to make restitution if he or she is unjustly enriched at the expense of another." (First Nationwide Savings v. Perry (1992) 11 Cal.App.4th 1657, 1662.)

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Off Calendar Sep-02-2021 Off Calendar

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-29-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT QUANG LE'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Le's motion pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 128.7 is denied.

Defendant did not comply with the safe harbor provision of Code of Civil Procedure section 128.7, which provides that the motion "shall not be filed with or presented to the court" unless the moving party first serves it on the opposing party at least 21 days before filing it. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 128.7(c)(1).) Thus, "A party seeking sanctions under section 128.7 follows a two-step procedure. First, the moving party serves notice of the motion for sanctions on the offending party. Service of the motion starts a safe harbor period during which the motion cannot be filed with the court. If the challenged paper, claim, defense, contention, allegation or denial is not withdrawn or corrected within a prescribed number of days, the second step is to file or present the motion for sanctions to the court." (Primo Hospitality Group, Inc. v. Haney (2019) 37 Cal.App.5th 165, 173-174.) Defendant served this motion June 23. (Def's Mt., pg. 10.) He filed this motion only 5 days later, on June 28.

To the extent Defendant is separately moving to strike the complaint, that motion is denied. First, "[a] motion for sanctions under this section shall be made separately from other motions or requests . . . ." (Code Civ. Proc. Section 128.7(c)(1).) Second, Defendant's motion fails to comply with the meet and confer rules pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 435.5(a).

Order signed.
